coredata.dc:title: Single, dual and multi-emission carbon dots based optosensing for food safety
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xuecheng
Jiang, Wei
Zhao, Yuan
Liu, Huilin
Sun, Baoguo
coredata.dc:description:
Background In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been increasingly studied owing to their excellent physical and optical properties. Conventional fluorescent sensing detection based on CDs usually relies on the intensity changes of a single fluorescent emission centre, which are often susceptible to interference due to changes in the concentration of sensor, uneven distribution, or the changes in instrument efficiency. Compared with a single fluorescent emission centre, dual-, triple-, and multi-fluorescent emission centres can overcome these obstacles and improve the robustness and accuracy of sensing by simultaneously exploiting multi-dimensional signal outputs.
Scope and approach This study focuses on the single-, dual- and multi-fluorescent emission centres of fabricated CDs and their emerging application in food safety detection. Furthermore, the multi-luminescence properties and photoluminescence mechanism will be discussed in depth. Particularly the applications of these materials are elaborated in regard to food quality and safety detection, including the detection of hazards due to food processing, pesticides, veterinary drug residues, toxins and metal ion. Finally, the current challenges and future perspectives will be presented in terms of fabricating CDs that enhance multi-dimensional optical properties for use in emerging biosensor applications.
Key findings and conclusions The dual-, and multi-fluorescent-emission centres of CDs coupled with ratiometric fluorescence, and multi-dimensional analysis, can effectively improve the sensitivity and accuracy of detection methods. Compared with the method of functionalizing the surface of CDs, and then connecting them with a corresponding fluorescent emitting material, the direct use of dual- and multi-fluorescent-emission CDs as the fluorescent material combined with chemometric analyses is a better choice for detecting substances in food matrices.

head.ce:title: Single, dual and multi-emission carbon dots based optosensing for food safety

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been increasingly studied owing to their excellent physical and optical properties. Conventional fluorescent sensing detection based on CDs usually relies on the intensity changes of a single fluorescent emission centre, which are often susceptible to interference due to changes in the concentration of sensor, uneven distribution, or the changes in instrument efficiency. Compared with a single fluorescent emission centre, dual-, triple-, and multi-fluorescent emission centres can overcome these obstacles and improve the robustness and accuracy of sensing by simultaneously exploiting multi-dimensional signal outputs.

This study focuses on the single-, dual- and multi-fluorescent emission centres of fabricated CDs and their emerging application in food safety detection. Furthermore, the multi-luminescence properties and photoluminescence mechanism will be discussed in depth. Particularly the applications of these materials are elaborated in regard to food quality and safety detection, including the detection of hazards due to food processing, pesticides, veterinary drug residues, toxins and metal ion. Finally, the current challenges and future perspectives will be presented in terms of fabricating CDs that enhance multi-dimensional optical properties for use in emerging biosensor applications.

The dual-, and multi-fluorescent-emission centres of CDs coupled with ratiometric fluorescence, and multi-dimensional analysis, can effectively improve the sensitivity and accuracy of detection methods. Compared with the method of functionalizing the surface of CDs, and then connecting them with a corresponding fluorescent emitting material, the direct use of dual- and multi-fluorescent-emission CDs as the fluorescent material combined with chemometric analyses is a better choice for detecting substances in food matrices.

ce:section.ce:para: With the global economic growth and the significant improvement in living standards, food safety issues in daily life still exist. It has become a major challenge facing the world. It not only causes great harm to human health, but also severely restrict import and export trade and industrial development. The public is paying more and more attention to the safety of food. In addition to the pathogens in the environment that can pollute food, heavy metals in water resources, hazards in food processing, various harmful pesticide residues in agricultural and sideline products or toxins in food can also cause food pollution and threaten food safety. Food safety analysis and detection are key technical means to ensure food safety. Traditional analytical detection methods such as gas chromatography, high performance liquid chromatography, gas-mass spectrometry and liquid-mass spectrometry, etc., although with high sensitivity and accuracy, there are also disadvantages such as relatively expensive equipment, long time consuming, relatively complex operation, and the need for professional and technical personnel, which cannot meet the needs of real-time and effective monitoring of the entire chain of food production, processing, circulation and sales (Choi, Yong, Choi, & Cowie, 2019; Shi et al., 2019). Therefore, in order to ensure food safety, there is an urgent need for sensitive, rapid, and accurate rapid detection of products, so that food safety detection technology is more diversified. Fluorescence-based detection methods have the advantages of simplicity, speed, portability, sensitivity, and low cost, and have gradually become a promising method for food safety analysis. However, typical fluorescent nanomaterials such as quantum dots (QDs) have high toxicity and poor biocompatibility (Zhou, Zou, Song, & Chen, 2018), heavy metal nanoclusters have low stability and high cost. These shortcomings limit their practical application in food safety analysis. Therefore, it is promising to develop fluorescent nanomaterials that can effectively make up for these shortcomings to construct a classic optical sensing system for food safety analysis.

Carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon nanodots or carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are zero-dimensional spherical nanoparticles with a particle size of less than 10 nm and have excellent fluorescence luminescent properties, which were first demonstrated in 2004 by Xu et al., when passivating single-walled carbon nanotubes by electrophoresis (X. Y. Xu et al., 2004). The structure of CDs is considered to be composed of a carbon core and functional groups on its surface. The carbon core is usually sp2 hybrid crystalline form or sp3 hybrid amorphous carbon structure, and the surface is composed of hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and other functional groups (Saberi, Rezaei, Rezaei, & Ensafi, 2020; J. P. Song et al., 2019; Zu et al., 2017). CDs have high photobleaching resistance, good water solubility, high chemical stability, easy synthesis routes, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility (An, Zhuo, Zhang, & Zhu, 2015; X. Miao et al., 2018; X. A. Miao et al., 2017). Due to these characteristics of CDs, the number of scientific publications about and citations referencing CDs have grown exponentially in past ten years (Fig. 1 A), indicating that the high expectations of CDs have received growing interest from the scientific community. We summarized the development of CDs from their discovery in 2004 (X. Y. Xu et al., 2004) (Fig. 1B). Nanoscale carbon particles were first named CDs in 2006 (Y. P. Sun et al., 2006). CDs with strong two-photon luminescence under the excitation of near-infrared pulsed lasers were prepared in 2007 (Cao et al., 2007). The first exploration of nitrogen-doped CDs was performed in 2012 (Y. Q. Zhang et al., 2012). CDs were first reported with the characteristic of room temperature phosphorescence in 2013 (Deng et al., 2013), and red-green dual-emissive CDs were prepared in 2018 (Liu et al., 2018).

In order to better highlight the fluorescence properties of CDs for future use, the size and surface functional group types of CDs can be strictly controlled and adjusted during the synthesis process (Qu, Wei, & Sun, 2018). According to the current report, CDs had made extensive developments in photoelectric conversion, photocatalysis, bioimaging systems and biosensing analysis by virtue of its excellent fluorescence characteristics. We have been actively involved in the field of CDs-based optical detection for many years (Zhang et al., 2019; Liu, Zhang, et al., 2019; Wei, Yuan, Liu, & Sun, 2020; Yuan, Liu, & Sun, 2020; Zhang et al., 2019; Dianwei et al., 2020), and have fabricated several important CD-based nanoparticles with good specificity and selectivity for use in food safety, fluorescence sensors based on CDs are used in various food safety detection systems to identify harmful products in food processing (e.g. biogenic amines, advanced glycosylation end products, pesticides and veterinary drug residues).

At present, most of the reported CDs-based optical sensing systems are in a state where the intensity of a single wavelength is tunable. Special atom doping or surface modification has made contributions to improving the fluorescence efficiency and detection sensitivity of CDs-based optical sensors. However, many factors such as changes in sensor concentration, uneven distribution or instrument efficiency, etc. often interfere with the quantitative results of detection (M. Amjadi & Jalili, 2017; R.; Long et al., 2020). Compared with single fluorescent emission centres, dual and triple fluorescent emission centres can overcome these obstacles and improve the robustness and accuracy of sensing detection by simultaneously exploiting the multi-dimensional signal outputs. In recent years, although a large number of reviews on CD synthesis methods, luminescence mechanisms and practical applications have been published, attention to the synthesis of CDs based on the number of fluorescent emission centres which used for optical detection is still scarce. Here, we focus on the construction method innovation of single-, dual- and multi-emission CDs based optical sensors (Fig. 1C) and their emerging applications in food safety. Furthermore, the multi-luminescence properties and photoluminescence mechanism will be discussed in depth. Finally, we discuss the current challenges and future perspectives on fabricating CDs with enhanced multi-dimensional optical properties for emerging biosensor applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of single-emission CDs based optosensing
ce:section.ce:para: The single-signal fluorescence detection of CDs is mainly based on the change of the fluorescence intensity of a single fluorescence emission peak of a single-emission CD under a single excitation wavelength to obtain the result. For the purpose of improving the sensitivity of this detection method, the construction strategy of single-emission CDs based optical sensor based traditional CDs, element-doped CDs, CD-based complex materials grafted with metal organic frameworks (MOFs) or covalent organic frameworks (COFs), combined with molecular imprinting technology (MIT) are introduced.

The synthesis of traditional single-emitting CDs is mostly dependent on different kinds of precursor materials. The prepared CDs exhibit obvious light absorption in the ultraviolet region. After the surface is passivated or the synthesis conditions are adjusted, the absorption wavelength and the fluorescence performance changed (Ankireddy & Kim, 2018; Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016; Gavahian, Chu, & Khaneghah, 2019; Li, Hong, Feng, & Sam, 2017; Liu, Zhang, et al., 2019). The optical sensing system based on such single-emitting CDs aims to cater to the development of the discipline, simplify the detection steps, and improve the detection sensitivity, and has always received greater attention. First of all, actively advocate the development of traditional green synthesis methods of CDs, and start to prepare blue single-emission fluorescent CDs by using orange peel as the precursor materials. When the constructed CDs based optical sensor detected Cr(VI) in water samples, the fluorescence quenching amount and target concentration follow the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)) strictly, and referring to the 3σ IUPAC standard (Eq. (2)), the limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 0.8 μM. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) is used as a chelating ligand to modify the surface of the CDs. The CDs@EDTA sensor system is constructed to emit fluorescence at 440 nm at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. 2 A), the presence of Cr(VI) caused the CDs to agglomerate and more obvious fluorescence quenching, the LOD is 10 nM.

where F0 is the fluorescence intensity without quencher, F is the fluorescence intensity with the addition of quencher, Ksv is the quenching constant and Q is the concentration of quencher.

where k is the slope of the linear calibration chart and σ is the standard deviation of the signal (n = 9) (Wang et al., 2020).

The surface of traditional single-emitting CDs is easily modified with different functional groups, and has great potential to adjust its physical and chemical properties, including their solubility, fluorescence properties, fluorescence quantum yield (QY), and build optical sensors to improve the selectivity and sensitivity of target detection. Using 3-pyridyl boronic acid as the sole carbon source to prepare CDs and realizing boronic acid functionalization on the surface of CDs. The constructed single-emission fluorescence detection system had good water solubility and specific recognition ability of sialic acid (SA) (S. Xu, Che, et al., 2019). The excellent acid oxidation process caused fluorescence resonance energy transfer and fluorescence quenching in the sensing system. Ethylene glycol diethyl ether diamine tetraacetic acid (EGTA) modified the surface of CDs to construct a biocompatible single-emission CDs based optical sensor with bright blue fluorescence for detecting intracellular Ca2+, with the concentration of Ca2+ increased, static fluorescence quenching occurred during the detection process, that is, EGTA is easily combined with Ca2+, leading to CDs aggregation and fluorescence quenching (Fig. 2B) (Yue et al., 2019). Surface passivation or modification optimizes the CDs-based optical sensing system in terms of functionality, and optical sensors based on long-wavelength fluorescent CDs with a single emission center that can overcome short-wave background interference have gradually become the current development trends. These CDs have been tested at 200 °C for pyrolyzing acrylamide and citric acid in formamide solvents and to propose their possible formation mechanism (Fig. 2C). First, the citric acid dissolved in formamide polymerized with acrylamide to form a cross-linked network intermediate, then the polycondensate was carbonized and formed a carbon core with a core functional group shell, resulting in CDs with red emission. The CDs showed a selective reaction to Hg2+ in a NaAc–HAc buffer solution. In addition, because Zn2+ could form a more stable complex with Hg2+ to restore the quenched fluorescence of these CDs, a CD-based “turn-off-on” fluorescence method was established for the simultaneous detection of Hg2+ and Zn2+ (Chen et al., 2020). At present, the preparation of traditional CDs is relatively simple and easy. With the gradual development of green synthesis methods, traditional single-emission CDs have become very promising identification elements for optical sensing systems. Optical sensors constructed are mostly based on the emission range and intensity of single-wavelength fluorescence to characterize the signal, thereby providing information about the identity, spatial position and concentration of the probe (Murugan, Prakash, Jayakumar, Sundaramurthy, & Sundramoorthy, 2019; H. Wen et al., 2019). Currently, the most studied single-emission fluorescent CDs emits blue or green fluorescence, with the demand for detection and application in biological systems, the construction of optical sensing systems based on high QY and long wavelength single emission CDs (orange or red fluorescent CDs) has become a trend. In addition, single-emitting CDs based optosensing can be developed as single-wavelength intensity adjustment “turn on” or “turn off” sensors, to detect biomarkers and biomolecules with excellent analytical performance at the same time.

Based on the natural fluorescence change of single emission CDs, CDs-based optical sensor is constructed for the quantitative analysis of substances, interacted with the target will cause the fluorescence quenching or enhancement of the sensing system. (X. Miao et al., 2018; (Zhu, Tan, Chen, Xiong, & Gao, 2019)). The doping of heteroatoms can provide CDs with enhanced fluorescence characteristics and higher selectivity. In recent years, reports on optical sensors based on heteroatom-doped CDs for substance detection have also been updated (Table 1 ), which has promoted the adoption and research of this construction strategy.

When heteroatoms are doped in the carbon skeleton of CDs, it will adjust the electronic characteristics and provide more active sites on the surface of CDs, including –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 (S. H. Miao et al., 2020; Qi et al., 2019; S. Wang, Yu, et al., 2020). These new sites can be used as energy traps and improve the fluorescent properties of the CDs. The reported heteroelements which introduced into the surface of CDs include non-metal elements, such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), boron (B), and phosphorus (P), and metal ions, such as manganese (Mn), copper (Cu), and magnesium (Mg). Among them, N has an adjustable atomic size, with five valence electrons. Furthermore, N can bond to carbon atoms to adjust the inherent characteristics of the CDs and improve their QY. Therefore, it is a good choice to choose high-quality carbon and nitrogen sources to construct single-emission heteroatom-doped CDs-based optical sensors (Xu et al., 2016). The fluorescent N-doped CDs with more adsorption sites on their surface by the co-preparation of ammonia and phyllanthus acidus, the N-CDs-based sensing system with QY (Eq. (3)) of 14% that can be used for the selective detection of Fe3+ with LOD of 0.9 μM (Atchudan et al., 2018).

where Qsam represents the QY of the CDs, Qref represents the QY of the reference sample, Asam represents the absorbance of the CDs at the excitation wavelength, Aref represents the absorbance of the reference sample, Isam represents the integrated fluorescence intensity of the CDs, Iref represents the integrated fluorescence intensity of the reference sample, nsam and nref are the refractive index of the CDs and reference solutions, respectively.

In addition, First to using sodium sulfide (Na2S) as the sulfur source and citric acid (CA) as the carbon precursor to synthesize S-doped CDs with blue fluorescence, while also proposed the possible growth process of S-CDs. Based on the reaction of CA and Na2S solution to generate H2S gas and deprotonated CA, then the desulfurized CA polymerized with sulfur ions to form the nucleus of S-CDs under high-temperature conditions, and finally, due to the presence of various active functional groups, S-CDs began to grow from the surface of the crystal nucleus (Fig. 3 A). Due to the doping of heteroatoms, the resulting S-CDs had a higher fluorescence intensity and fluorescence QY and a stronger stability than undoped CDs. When the single-emission S-CDs-based optical sensor system constructed with the above method and used to detect Hg2+, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of S-CDs reacted with Hg2+ in a complicated manner, which lead to its re-polymerizationunderwent to increase the non-radiative transition of electrons and decrease the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system (Fig. 3B), this process enabled highly sensitive and selective detection (Wang et al., 2019). In 2020, a study conducted intermolecular condensation of 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) to form a polymer, and then carbonized the polymer to obtain Cl-doped CDs with carboxylic acid groups and phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface. In the normalized UV–vis spectrum, it was found that there were three typical absorption bands at 225, 276 and 325 nm approximately, which were due to the π→π* transition of the aromatic ring, the n→π* transition of CO and the capture of excited-state energy by the surface state, respectively. The Cl-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under irradiation with 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. Based on the advantages of these functional groups on the surface of Cl-CDs that could capture Fe3+, the fluorescence of the Cl-CDs-based optical sensor was quenched by internal filtering to achieve the quantitative detection of Fe3+ (Fig. 3C) (Wang et al., 2020). In addition to non-metallic doping, the metal-atom doping can improve the optical performance of CDs, the constructed metal-atom doped CDs-based optical sensor will have more novel functions due to modulating the combined structure (Q. Xu, Y. Liu et al., 2016). For example, Qing et al. treated copper acetate monohydrate (Cu(Ac)2·H2O) and waste tea extract by a hydrothermal carbonization to obtain water-soluble Cu-doped CDs, the fluorescence could be specifically quenched by Cr(VI) and were not interfered with other common ions, this type of Cu-CDs-based optical sensing system were successfully applied to the detection of Cr(VI) in actual samples (Qing, Chen, Yang, Wang, & Liu, 2020).

Single-atom doped CDs have better fluorescence properties, additionally, the single-atom doping method has many advantages and more potential in restricting the properties of the CDs themselves. However, some limitations are still inevitable, For example, for reported single-doped CDs, the emission spectrum of the constructed single-emission optical sensor is mostly limited to the yellow-blue range. In recent years, there have been reported that the strategy of multi-atomic co-doping can effectively improve these shortcomings. Since the synergistic effect between heteroatoms doped in CDs can produce a special electronic structure, the simultaneous co-doping of multiple atoms opens up new possibilities for further changing the overall photoelectric performance of CDs and the construction of optical sensing systems (Atchudan et al., 2017; Pakkath et al., 2018).

Recently, CDs co-doped with N and S have been actively studied by many research groups, which is an ideal way to enhance the photoluminescence characteristics. For example, Martyna used citric acid and thiourea as precursor materials, obtained N, S-CDs by microwave synthesis. After investigating the optical properties of N, S-CDs and obtaining their fluorescence spectrum, the N,S-CDs-based optical sensor with the best excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of 358 nm and 436 nm (Fig. 4 A) was constructed to detect Hg2+ quickly, with significant fluorescence quenching effect and high sensitivity (Pajewska-Szmyt, Buszewski, & Gadzala-Kopciuch, 2020). In addition, to expand the application possibilities of multi-atom co-doped CDs-based optical sensors, other atoms, such as B, Cl, and Se, have also been used in co-doped structures. For example, based on the effect that the B atom of sp3 in the phenylboronic acid group can form a coordination bond with Fe3+, B, N co-doped CDs were designed and an optical sensor was constructed. Unsurprisingly, under the interference of other common metal ions, this system had a higher selectivity to Fe3+, and the most likely quenching mechanism was that the B in the B, N-CDs functionalized by boric acid groups in the sensing system could easily combine with hydroxyl groups in water to form sp3 hybridized B atoms. These boric acid groups contained three oxygen atoms to coordinate with Fe3+, and Fe3+ would coordinate with the hydroxyl group and form a non-fluorescent complex, thus, when the B, N-CDs in the system move in the solution, it would collide with Fe3+ in the solution, that is, Fe3+ would greatly quench the fluorescence intensity of the B,N-CDs-based optical sensor through static and dynamic synergy (Fig. 4B) (H. Wu, Pang, Fu, Guo, & Wang, 2020). In 2019, Yang and others used aloe as a raw material, while urea and FeCl3·6H2O were obtained as nitrogen sources and Cl sources, respectively. Then, these materials were combined to form N,Cl-co-doped CDs and constructed N,Cl-CDs based optical sensor. Based on the mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer, the target analyte that is tartrazine assumed the role of an energy acceptor, and the doped CDs in the system acted as energy donors, therefore, the interaction between the two caused fluorescence quenching and achieved the effective detection of tartrazine (Fig. 4C) (Yang et al., 2020). In the same year, Hu et al. realized the synthesis of yellow luminous Se,N co-doped CDs with o-phenylenediamine and selenium urea as precursors and its QY is 16.7% in aqueous solution. The constructed Se, N-CDs-based optical sensor can quickly detect crystal violet (CV). At the same time, it is believed that due to the internal filter effect, the inherent yellow fluorescence could be quenched by CV (Hu & Gao, 2020).

In summary, heteroatom doping is a fast and simple method to adjust the fluorescence characteristics of CDs and construct an effective single-emission CDs-based optical sensing system, but due to the lack of basic understanding of the correlation between the structure and optical properties, controlling the photophysical properties of luminescent CDs by heteroatom doping is still challenging. In addition, most of the precursors of doped CDs have high chemical costs and are harmful to the environment. Therefore, the development of simpler synthesis methods and environmentally friendly sources to obtain doped CDs with high photoluminescence and to construct optical sensors with excellent detection performance have broad application prospects.

In 1995, the research group of Yaghi reported the first MOFs, and gave them their name. These compounds have a two-dimensional structure and are synthesized by a planar rigid ligand (isophthalic acid) and a transition metal (Co) containing empty d orbitals. The publication of this article has made the synthesis of MOFs gradually become a new and promising direction in the field of chemistry (Yaghi, Li, & Li, 1995). Later research found that MOFs are a kind of porous crystalline material composed of the interaction and assembly of organic ligands and central metal ions. The main difference of MOFs from other compounds is that they have metal-carbon bonds. Therefore, MOFs-based materials have a number of promising characteristics, such as high specific surface area, desirable structure and composition, channels that can be adjusted and functionalized (Amini, Kazemi, & Safarifard, 2020; Guo et al., 2017; X. J.; Guo et al., 2019). Among functional MOFs, luminescence-functionalized MOFs may have various tunable optical properties, and have been used as a promising sensory material to detect metal ions, organic molecules, and explosives. However, it may be that it is difficult to obtain MOFs with a high luminescence QY due to the weak luminescence of metal-ligand charge transfer in MOFs or the inherent luminescence of the organic and inorganic components (J. P. Song et al., 2019). Therefore, in the past decades, MOFs and its derived nanocomposites, such as optical sensing systems constructed by grafting with CDs, have made notable progress for application in the field of substance detection.

While maximizing the structure and characteristics of the host MOFs, a luminescent guest material with a high QY, namely, CDs, can be introduced into the MOFs. By using the excellent sensing selectivity of CDs with a high luminescence QY, while also using the host MOFs for its highly selective adsorption and effective accumulation of target substances, the single-emission optical sensor constructed by this method can achieve the adjustable and selective detection of analytes. For example, Lin and colleagues developed an encapsulation strategy to incorporate highly dispersed branched poly (ethylenimine)-capped CDs (BPEI-CDs) into a prepared zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), ZIF-8 is an MOF based on zeolite imidazole with a large cavity and small pore size. The BPEI-CDs-ZIF-8 based optical sensor not only had excellent fluorescence activity and sensing selectivity attributed to the CDs, but could also strongly and selectively accumulate the target analyte, thereby achieving an ultra-sensitive and highly selective detection of Cu2+ (X. M. Lin, Gao, Zheng, Chi, & Chen, 2014). Similarly, CDs with high QY are used as guest fluorescent materials to be grafted onto ZIF-8 that used as an adsorbent to selectively enrich and capture analytes to construct a CDs-ZIF-8-based optical sensor system, where the blue light-emitting CDs acted as tentacles to sensitively recognize the bond interaction between the analyte and ZIF-8 (Fig. 5 A). The constructed optical sensor showed excellent fluorescence selectivity to quercetin (QCT), with an LOD of only 3.5 nM (L. H. Xu, Fang, et al., 2016). Recently, the post-synthesis modification method (PSMA) has become another strategy for building functional composite materials based on MOFs. Using PSMA to load other functional materials into MOFs can provide enhanced performance with unique properties that cannot be obtained alone. For example, zirconium-based MOFs that is UiO-66 and amine-functionalized CDs (amine-CDs) were integrated through PSMA. UiO-66, which is Zr6O4(OH)4(bdc)6 (bdc = benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid benzene), is a representative porous MOF, and the porosity of this MOF was studied by measuring the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77 K. In addition, it was found that the specific surface area and average channel size of the obtained amine-CDs@UiO-66 composite were smaller than those of the pure UiO-66 material. The arrangement of amine-CD nanocrystals in the pore channel of the amine-CDs@UiO-66 composite led to a decrease in the micropore volume and channel diameter (Fig. 5B). The prepared single-emission optical sensor detected 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) with trace amounts and had a low LOD in the linear region of 0.01–20 μM. Additionally, the enrichment of 4-NP using the well-defined MOFs micropores significantly improved the fluorescence sensitivity. Due to the limitation of the principle of internal molecular rotation, the unique rigid linker in MOFs can enhance fluorescence by efficiently adjusting the electronic transition, so CDs can activate the fluorescence of an MOF and provide opportunities for it to be used as a fluorescent probe (Yang, Hu, Liu, & Zhang, 2019). Based on this, researches on incorporating CDs into MOFs to form CDs/MOFs materials and constructing CDs/MOFs-based optical sensors are still underway, also the enhanced fluorescence which is generated through the hydrogen-bonds between the amino group of the MOF and the hydroxyl group of CDs. When applied to the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+, their presence will cause the CDs to separate from the MOFs, and the fluorescence will be quenched, showed a “turn off” switching status (Fig. 5C) (Fan, Wang, Li, & Zhou, 2020). Therefore, the sensitivity and selectivity of the CDs/MOFs-based optical sensing system can be improved at the later stage by using the fluorescence activation of CDs or other competitive mechanisms. Based on the coordinated relationship, MOFs and CDs are combined to form a “turn off-turn on” fluorescent probe to detect other metal ions. In addition, it is necessary to develop CDs/MOFs-based optical sensor system that have little toxicity to cells and can detect various ions in cells. In general, regarding the application of MOFs in chemistry, its sensing technology has not attracted much attention because the development of MOFs for sensing applications is relatively difficult. It is difficult to carry out effective signal transmission in the system and continues to remain a challenge.

In 2005, Yaghi et al. reported the first case of COFs, which is a class of crystalline organic porous materials formed by organic structural units connected by covalent bonds (Côté et al., 2005). Compared with other amorphous organic porous materials, COFs have the characteristics of adjustable functionalization, a regular pore structure and a high surface area. Researchers have designed and synthesized organic components through a large variety of organic synthesis methods and designed the framework of COFs through framework chemistry to realize the directional construction and functionalization of COFs (Bhadra et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2016). Currently, functional COFs can amplify fluorescence changes through its extended π-conjugated framework to achieve selective sensing for various applications (Gao et al., 2018). The introduction of luminescent groups into COFs to obtain COFs with good luminescent properties is a common method in sensing applications. In 2016, Ding et al. functionalized the sulfide of a COF material to obtain COF-LZU8 and successfully achieved the selective detection of Hg2+. The powder-coloured crystal structure of COF-LZU8 was suggested by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) (Fig. 6 A) (Ding et al., 2016). Later, Wang and his colleagues used tetrahedrons and planar quadrilaterals as precursors and successfully constructed the first three-dimensional fluorescent COF (Fig. 6B) through the [4 + 4] condensation reaction. As the concentration of picric acid increased, the fluorescence quenching effect of the COF increased gradually (G. Q. Lin, Ding, Yuan, Wang, & Wang, 2016). In recent years, with the extensive attention and continuous innovation of researchers on luminescent CDs, there have been works to peel off and cut a covalent organic skeleton, based on covalent triazine (CTF-1) with a layered structure, with a strongly oxidizing piranha solution to obtain N-doped CDs and construct a single-emitting CDs-based optical sensor with high sensitivity and high selectivity (Fig. 6C). The obtained CDs were used as a photoluminescence probe to quickly and sensitively detect Hg2+. This work opened up a new way for the use of COFs as raw materials to develop new CDs that applied to the sensing detection (Y. Z. Zhu et al., 2017).

The single-emission optical sensor based on MIT and single-emission fluorescent CDs realized rapid visualization and qualitative or quantitative detection of target analytes. However, the established visual sensor analysis method only exhibited changes in fluorescence intensity. More fluorescent materials with stable performance and absorption wavelengths in the visible region should be sought, and the integration of MIT and new technologies, such as nanomaterials should be strengthened to provide an effective basis for the realization of automated rapid detection technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of dual-emission CDs based optosensing
ce:section.ce:para: Optical sensors based on dual emission CDs mostly exist in the form of ratiometric fluorescence analysis, which mainly monitors the fluorescence response changes of the two fluorescence emission peaks that vary with the concentration of target analytes, and performs quantitative detection based on the ratio. Compared with the single-signal fluorescence analysis method, the risk of false positives in this detection process is significantly reduced and the detection sensitivity can be significantly improved (Pennacchio et al., 2016; W.; Song et al., 2017).

The basis of the ratiometric fluorescence method with a self-calibration function is to determine the ratio of the fluorescence intensity at two well-resolved emission peaks, which can effectively avoid external interference such as background absorption and eliminate light source fluctuations (Su et al., 2018; Y. Q.; Sun et al., 2018; C. X.; Wang, Lin, et al., 2016). In addition, the key to ratiometric fluorescence measurements is always the design and preparation of dual-emission fluorescent probes. Therefore, a series of synthetic methods for dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probes have been reported and developed. Most ratiometric fluorescent probes are obtained by linking or embedding fluorescent materials in a fluorescent or non-fluorescent matrix. As a rising star of fluorescent nanomaterials, CDs have many attractive characteristics, and many reports have described the combination of CDs with other materials to achieve the ratiometric fluorescence detection and analysis of analytes.

Luminescent MOFs, as promising new type of microporous hybrid crystalline materials, have attracted increasing attention in terms of sensing small organic molecules and inorganic ions. Currently, most of the reported optical detection methods based on luminescent MOFs only provide monochromatic light signals and intensity changes, which are not conducive to signal analysis with the naked eye (C. X. Wang, Lin, et al., 2016). Based on this observation, a red-emitting MOF (Eu-MOF) encapsulating nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N, S-CD) with strong blue emission have been studied to obtain dual-emission nanohybrids (Eu-MOFs/N, S-CD) with two colours. The Eu-MOFs/N,S-CD composite material exhibited red light emission in an organic solvent and blue light emission in water. With the increased of water content in ethanol solution, the blue emission intensity increased, and the red emission peak decreased. Based on this photoluminescence characteristic, colorimetric sensing detection was realized. When used as a detection probe for the water content in organic solvents, it was found that the ratio of light intensity at 420 nm and 623 nm (I420/I623) increased linearly with the increase of water content in the range of 0.05%–4% and the LOD was 0.03% (Fig. 8 A) (Y. Q. Dong, Cai, Fang, You, & Chi, 2016). Similarly, in 2017, Wu developed a ratiometric fluorescence method based on red CDs and blue zirconium MOF that is UiO-66-(COOH)2 for the highly selective detection of Cu2+ in aqueous solution. Their interesting finding was that the –NH2 remaining on the surface of the prepared CD could effectively coordinate Cu2+ to produce strong visible light absorption, which overlapped with the emission of UiO-66-(COOH)2. In a practical sense, the red fluorescence emission of CD increased slightly with an increasing concentration of Cu2+, and the blue fluorescence emission of UiO-66-(COOH)2 was clearly weakened (J.-X. Wu & Yan, 2017).

As another new type of fluorescent nanomaterial, metal nanoclusters (NCs) have received increasing attention. Metal NCs are composed of a few to nearly a hundred atoms with diameter of less than 10 nm (Peng et al., 2015). Compared with other fluorescent nanoparticles, NCs have higher light stability and a larger Stokes shift (Shamsipur, Molaabasi, Hosseinkhani, & Rahmati, 2016). Similar to CDs, metal NCs synthesized with different ligands which have different emission characteristics. Thus, their use is feasible for the selective and sensitive fluorescent chemical sensing and biosensing systems. Therefore, research has used the electrostatic interaction between CuNCs and CDs to prepare a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe to achieve the sensitive detection of sulfide. The obtained CuNCs-CDs composite material showed two different emission wavelengths corresponding to the CDs (469 nm) and CuNCs (622 nm) at a single excitation wavelength and exhibited high sensitivity to sulfides. With an increasing sulfide concentration, the emission peak at 622 nm showed a decreasing trend, while the peak at 469 nm remained basically unchanged. When the concentration of sulfide increased to 1 ppm, the fluorescence peak corresponding to CuNCs basically disappeared, I622/I469 had a good linear relationship with the sulphide concentration from 2 to 10 ppb, and the LOD was as low as 4.3 nM (Fig. 8B) (Wen et al., 2019). Additionally, Babaee and colleagues used CDs with blue emitting and (Ag/Au)@insulin(Ins)NCs with red emitting to obtain an innovative dual-emission ratiometric nano-hybridization probe to measure Hg2+ and Cu2+. In the presence of analytes, the red-emitting NCs (λex = 340 nm, λem = 640 nm) were strongly quenched by Cu2+ and Hg2+, and the fluorescence intensity of CD (λex = 340 nm, λem = 420 nm) was not affected (Babaee et al., 2019).

In addition to the combination of CDs and other luminescent materials, MIT is also used in the construction of dual-emission ratiometric nano-hybridization probes. Recently, Jalili incorporated blue luminescent CDs (B-CDs) into silica balls. Then, a mesoporous imprinted polymer layer embedded with yellow luminescent CDs (Y-CDs) on the surface of B-CDs@SiO2 was obtained by the sol-gel method (Fig. 8C). The obtained nanocomposite had two different emission peaks at 560 and 440 nm at a single excitation wavelength. These emission peaks were used as the response and reference signals, respectively. When penicillin-G (PNG) was added as the analyte, the blue fluorescence fluctuated slightly and the yellow fluorescence gradually disappeared. The resulting fluorescence intensity ratio could be used for the trace level determination of PNG (Jalili, Khataee, Rashidi, & Razmjou, 2020).

In order to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the measurement of various indicators or ingredients in food safety analysis, the traceability, comparability and validity of data results, chemometric analysis has gradually become a basic dependence. The ratiometric nanosensor encapsulated by biological local embedding, which is constructed by encapsulating an intensity-based probe and an inert reference fluorescent substance in a stable matrix, has the advantages of easy synthesis and anti-interference. Taking into account the strict selection requirements of inert reference phosphors, a dual-wavelength monitoring strategy and the quantitative fluorescence model for generalized ratiometric probes (QFMGRP) were used to study the feasibility of using CDs as an inert reference. The results showed that the combination of QFMGRP model, dual-wavelength monitoring strategy and dual-emission CDs based optical sensing system can accurately quantify NO2− and free Ca2+ in actual samples. This strategy has general applicability in the construction of ratiometric optical sensing system and its quantitative application (Yan, Chen, Huang, Kang, & Yu, 2019).

According to current reports, due to abundant copper reserves and relatively low prices, the cost of preparing Cu NCs is much lower than the cost of preparing silver and gold NCs. Therefore, a hybrid material composed of Cu NCs and CDs is expected to become an ideal fluorescence ratiometric probe. In addition, on the basis of previous research, further processing of NCs and CDs to construct a ratiometric fluorescent test paper is a research direction that can be implemented in the future. While MIT guarantees the selectivity of the detection process, the ratiometric sensing method and mesoporous structure of an imprinted shell increase the detection sensitivity, therefore, MIT shows broad prospects for application as ratiometric nano-hybridization probes. It cannot be ignored that it is also necessary to develop a dual-emission CDs based optical sensing system combined with more chemometric analysis to ensure the credibility of the construction process or data resources.

According to current reports, most ratiometric fluorescent probes usually require two or more luminescent substances to achieve dual-signal detection. However, embedded fluorescent materials are prone to leak from the matrix, and the complicated design preparation and purification process along with their high cost limit their further application (Cui, Liu, Ye, & Zhao, 2019; Xiang et al., 2018). Therefore, it is extremely meaningful to simply prepare a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on dual emission CDs. To date, reports on dual-emission CDs based optical sensing systems have mostly focused on the detection process of a single target substance, reports on the simultaneous detection of two or more target substances is still limited.

Using dual-emission CDs as luminescent materials for ratiometric fluorescence detection, the prepared detection tool will provide its own built-in calibration function to minimize its environmental impact (Cui et al., 2019; H. L. Liu, Zhang, et al., 2019). Based on two well-resolved wavelengths, the detection and quantification of a single-target substance is performed by measuring the ratio of the calculated fluorescence intensity (Shangguan et al., 2016). With the unique properties of dual-emission CDs, the detection of a single-target analyte will have advantages in terms of accuracy and sensitivity. In 2019, o-phenylenediamine and 2-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde were used as precursor materials, and a dual-emission fluorescent CD was successfully obtained. The prepared CD had good selectivity and anti-interference ability along with a fast response time. Additionally, the detection of arginine (Arg) was achieved by the ratiometric fluorescence method in a 100% aqueous solution (Fig. 9 A) ((Wang et al., 2019). In the same year, and in line with current research trends and concerns, Guan and his colleagues used one-step solvothermal method to synthesize N,S co-doped dual-emission CDs. Due to the addition of heteroatoms, the obtained dual-emission CDs had the characteristics of a high graphitization degree and low oxidation degree. In addition, its unique dual-luminescence characteristics and excitation independence made it have application value for the detection of water samples in ethanol solutions (Guan et al., 2019). Recently, it was discovered that the complex components in natural biomass could make the prepared CDs contain different surface functional groups with special sensing characteristics. Therefore, CDs using natural biomass as a precursor may have dual-fluorescence emission capabilities. There are studies that have used white pepper powder, due to its abundance of natural alkaloids and porphyrin derivatives as a raw material for the preparation of CDs. Notably, a dual-emission CD that emits green light at 520 nm and red light at 668 nm is obtained. It is found that green light (520 nm) can act as an efficient and special “turn on” fluorescence sensor, and the change in red light (668 nm) can be ignored. In the concentration range of 0–150 μM, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F520/F668) exhibited an excellent linear correlation with the concentration of coenzyme A (CoA) (Fig. 9B) (Long et al., 2020). In addition, a fluorescent pH nanoprobe based on the internal proportional label-free and self-calibration characteristics of a dual-emission CD was studied. This work used o-phenylenediamine as the carbon and nitrogen source, polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a surface passivator for the CD, and oxalic acid (OA) to provide the CDs with oxygen-rich groups. Finally, the inherent dual-emission characteristic of the CDs at a single excitation wavelength effectively avoided the use of complex devices and was an excellent ratiometric luminescent nanoprobe for the monitor of pH (Cao et al., 2019).

Recently, the development of new fluorescent probes for simultaneously distinguishing two different targets from a single dual-emission CD has received considerable attention because this detection strategy can provide more reliable information for biomedical or biological analysis. Moreover, the simultaneous detection that occurs with dual-target and even multi-target analyses also improves the detection efficiency to a certain extent, and these forms of analysis are beneficial for rapid and instant detection (Santhakumar et al., 2018; Zou et al., 2016). Although some dual-emission CDs have been reported, there are still few methods for generating CDs with wavelength-resolved dual-target sensing capabilities. Recently, Song et al. used one-pot method to treat black tea in an acetone solvent to obtain a fluorescent CDs with dual-emission characteristics. The prepared CDs were characterized by various techniques and found to have a weak blue fluorescence emission peak at 478 nm and a strong red fluorescence emission peak at 671 nm at an excitation wavelength of 410 nm. In addition, Cu2+ clearly quenched the fluorescence of the red emission band of the CDs and eventually caused this fluorescence to disappear completely. Furthermore, the addition of Al3+ significantly increased the fluorescence of the blue emission band. Based on this, a dual-emission CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors that could simultaneously detect Cu2+ and Al3+ was developed (J. P. Song et al., 2019). Another study prepared a novel dual-emission CD by one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method for the ratiometric detection of dynamic changes in lysine and pH in living cell systems. The prepared CDs showed two different fluorescence emission peaks at 440 and 624 nm at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The difference of the dual-emission CDs from previous CDs is that the CDs based sensor exhibited an interesting wavelength-dependent dual-response function for pH (624 nm) and lysine (440 nm). Lysine enhanced the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm of the dual-emission CDs and maintained a stable fluorescence intensity at 624 nm. Thus, a reliable ratiometric sensing detection method was constructed in the “turn on” mode (W. Song et al., 2017). Wang et al. developed a method to directly prepare dual-emission CDs by carbonizing GSH in a mixture of different proportions of water and formamide at 180 °C. The CD had emission wavelengths at 655 nm and 470 nm at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. With the increase in the ratio of water/formamide, the prepared CD showed a tendency to have only a single fluorescence emission peak, so a small amount of water might be beneficial to the production of dual-emission CDs. In addition, it was found that the add of Fe3+ with environment of pH < 7.0, the fluorescence emission peak at 655 nm was significantly quenched. In contrast, when Zn2+ was added in an environment of pH > 7.0, the fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm was quenched. Based on this, the prepared dual-emission CDs was used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ and Zn2+ in serum samples (Fig. 9C) (Wang, Lao, Ding, Zhang, & Liu, 2019).

Compared with single-emission CDs, dual-emission CDs with dual-fluorescence emission peaks at a single excitation wavelength have promising development prospects. First, dual-emission CDs can be directly used as a ratiometric sensor to overcome the interference of environmental factors and background signals affected by the object to be measured, thereby improving the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection method. On the other hand, the colour tone of the system is changed by adjusting the fluorescence intensity emitted in the visible light region, which greatly broadens the colour change range of the visual sensor. However, due to the low fluorescence QY of some dual-emission CDs, the fluorescence visualization effect when detecting analytes with UV lamp is not obvious enough. Therefore, more work in the future may focus on the synthesis of high-QY dual-emission fluorescent CDs and the construction of corresponding optical sensing systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of multi-emission CDs based optosensing
ce:section.ce:para: Although the ratiometric fluorescence analysis method based on CDs has many advantages, there are few studies on the luminescence mechanism of this type of fluorescent emission. Moreover, the intensity of two emission peaks of the reported dual-emission CDs or dual-emission CDs-based materials cannot be controllably adjusted, which can easily cause the combined fluorescence colour of the two fluorescence peaks displayed by the blank probe and to be unusable for ratiometric detection (Cai et al., 2018; Y. Q.; Dong et al., 2016; J.; Zhang & Yu, 2016). In addition, the visual discolouration range established by two fluorescence emission peaks is still not wide enough (Cai et al., 2018; X. Y.; Wang, Yu, et al., 2020), so fluorescence analysis detection adjusted by multiple fluorescence emission peaks has great development potential.

Multi-emission CDs are CDs with three or more fluorescence emissions at a single excitation wavelength. When constructing a fluorescent probe for substance detection, multiple fluorescence detection signal spectra may appear. After identifying the analyte, the multiple fluorescence emission intensity will increase or decrease in different trends to overcome the interference of unrelated factors. In addition, the colour of multiple fluorescence will be mutagenized with the change in fluorescence intensity to better achieve the degree of fluorescence visualization detection (J. Zhang & Yu, 2016). At present, there are few reports on the preparation of multi-emission CDs by a one-pot method and the construction of multi-emission CDs based optical sensing system and its application in actual sample analysis. Most studies have only combined CDs with other luminescent materials to obtain multi-emission fluorescent complexs, which were then used them for optical detection. For example, there is a research on mixing blue-emission CDs (bCDs), green-emission quantum dots (gQDs) and red-emission quantum dots (rQDs) that do not interfere with each other to form a three-colour optical sensor. Therefore, the fluorescence of the gQDs and rQDs are quenched by Cu2+ at the same time, and the bCDs of the colour changing background are not sensitive to Cu2+, thereby acting as a reliable reference signal (Fig. 10 A). When different amounts of Cu2+ were added, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity of the three-colour probe was constantly changing (Fig. 10B), so bCDs, gQDs and rQDs at a volume ratio of 3:4:9 were mixed to prepare a portable fluorescent test paper to achieve the rapid and sensitive visual monitoring of Cu2+ in human urine. This test paper provided a clear way to judge whether the Cu2+ levels in human urinary tracts were abnormal for later clinical diagnosis (Fig. 10C) (Cai et al., 2018).

Overall, the multi-peak signal response can more effectively overcome the interference of factors unrelated to the analyte, with high sensitivity, a fast response time, and a stable response. However, to date, the development of multi-fluorescence analysis methods based on CDs is far from sufficient. Not only do we have to make more efforts in the research of synthesizing multi-emission CDs in one pot, but meeting the goals of sensitive multi-colour sensing with simple steps remains challenge. In addition, based on the combination of dual-emission CDs based optical sensing system and chemometric analysis, efforts can be made gradually in the multi-emission CDs based optical sensor. For example, with the high sensitivity and accuracy of this sensing system, simultaneously identify and detect a variety of similar substances, and through a combination of classical partial least square discriminant analysis (PLSDA), all detection results are distinguished with high sensitivity, specificity and satisfactory recognition rate (Hu et al., 2018). Using soft modeling such as multivariate curve resolution (MCR-ALS) to perform chemometric preprocessing on the fluorescence data set of the multi-emission CDs based optical sensing system. This is a key step to turn these nanocomposite materials with interesting fluorescent properties into analytically useful nanosensors (Leitão, Tauler, & Silva, 2011). Effectively expanding the combination of traditional fluorescence and chemometric analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CDs for food safety detection
ce:section.ce:para: It is concluded that the application of CDs-based optical sensors in food safety analysis (Table 3 ), including hazards in food processing, pesticides and veterinary drug residues, toxins, and metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hazards in food processing
ce:section.ce:para: In the process of food processing, hazards will be produced due to external conditions and processing technology. Typical hazards include biogenic amines, heterocyclic amines and other components. It is essential to determine hazards in food processing and CDs are a promising material for application in this field. The International Agency for Research on Cancer regards some heterocyclic aromatic amines as possible human carcinogens, and these can be widely found in grilled, barbecued or fried meat and fish and in urine samples. Therefore, a key point in food safety is cooked food, which is the main source of the above proteins being formed because of their incomplete combustion processes at high temperatures. López et al. prepared CDs from lactose by a microwave process, with the advantages of a fast preparation method and good fluorescence efficiency, reproducibility and stability. Furthermore, the exploitation of the sensory ability of these CDs, without any further functionalization, gives rise to them being an excellent tool for the quantification of four different heterocyclic aromatic amines by their quenching effect on the fluorescence of CDs (Lopez et al., 2015). Histamine is primarily found in spoiled food and is often used as an indicator of food safety. Shi et al. developed a selective and sensitive fluorescence biosensor based on CDs and synthetic peptides for histamine detection in fish samples. Their one-pot hydrothermal treatment produced CDs whose fluorescence was effectively quenched by peptides via electron transfer interactions. In the presence of histamine, the fluorescence intensity recovered due to the stronger interaction between histamine and the peptides (R. Shi et al., 2020).

At present, researches on the construction of single-emission CDs-based optical sensors for the detection of hazardous substances in food processing, whether it is nitroso compounds, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons or heterocyclic amines, require excellent trace amounts and quick detection capability, dual or multiple-emission CDs can be used as potential optical sensing elements to improve detection accuracy, while effectively avoiding the influence of various external factors on the detection data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of pesticides and veterinary drug residues
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the extensive use of pesticides and veterinary drugs, crops or meat products often contain a small amount of pesticide and veterinary drug residues. Long-term ingestion of pesticide and veterinary drug residues seriously affects human health. Transfer to the ecosystem will also severely restrict environmental friendliness. The harmful effects of serotonin require more accurate and selective detection methods. Therefore, pesticide or veterinary drug residue control is a key point in food safety. Nagarajan et al. used amine-functionalized cellulose sponges coated on CDs for the separation and detection of the toxic herbicide atrazine. The maximum adsorption capacity of the prepared CD complex was found to be 32.06 mg/g, and the sorption satisfactorily followed pseudo-first-order kinetics and the Freundlich isotherm model (Nagarajan, Varada, & Venkatanarasimhan, 2020). Shekarbeygia et al. studied the extraction methods of rose pigments on the optical properties of CDs. Afterwards, a sensitive nanosensor based on the fluorescence quenching of the as-prepared CDs was prepared for the determination of diazinon in water (Shekarbeygi, Farhadian, Khani, Moradi, & Shahlaei, 2020). Based on the redox ability of iron ions, in which the fluorescence intensity of CDs was proportional to the pesticide concentration, a universal method for pesticide detection was established by Lin et al. (B. X. Lin et al., 2018). Wang et al. prepared an aptasensor for kanamycin detection using gold nanoparticles as an absorber to quench CD fluorescence via the inner filter effect. This strategy mainly relied on the fact that the absorption spectra of gold nanoparticles overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectra of the fluorophores as well as the specific binding capacity of the Ky2 aptamer to kanamycin. Upon adding the kanamycin antibiotic, the free aptamer sequences were first exhausted to form complexes, which led to gold nanoparticle aggregation at high salt concentrations. Consequently, the absorption spectrum of the adsorbent changes and no longer overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs, which results in the obvious fluorescence recovery of the aptasensor. Under optimal conditions, the aptasensor can detect kanamycin with a limit of detection as low as 18 nM (J. Wang, Lu, et al., 2020). Maria et al. developed a rapid optosensing method with high sensitivity for the simultaneous detection of amantadine and chloramphenicol residues in chicken breast. This duplex and homogeneous fluorescent immunoassay was based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between polyethyleneimine-functionalized blue- and green-emissive CDs and nanosheets. The specific immunoreaction could trigger the efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer between the donors and acceptors, causing the fluorescence quenching of the CDs (B. L. Dong, Li, Sun, Mari, et al., 2020). A series of optical sensors was developed based on environmentally friendly MIPs coated on CDs for the rapid and efficient detection of thiabendazole (Kazemifard et al., 2020). Sulfur- and nitrogen-doped CDs exhibited high stability in aqueous solution and were applied for the fluorometric detection of tetracyclines by the inner filter effect mechanism. These S,N-CDs were successfully utilized for the detection of residual tetracyclines in fish and pork samples with an acceptable recovery and a good RSD (S. Wang, Yu, et al., 2020). Furthermore, the enzymatically formed products of horseradish peroxidase/alkaline phosphatase efficiently quenched the CDs via the inner filter effect, thereby improving the sensitivity of conventional ELISA. The absorption signal of the conventional ELISA was converted into the fluorescence signal. Thus, a fluorescent immunoassay was successfully developed and used to detect amantadine residues in chicken, achieving a limit of detection of 0.02 ng/mL (B. L. Dong et al., 2019). Our group prepared nitrogen-doped CDs derived from COFs embedded in MIPs by reverse microemulsion polymerization. This CD-based complex was used as a potential candidate for the optosensing of flonicamid in cucumber and apple samples by combining the merits of superior physicochemical properties due to the high stability of N-doped CDs, an efficient fluorescence-resonance charge-transfer strategy, and highly specific and sensitive MIT (X. Yuan, Liu, & Sun, 2020).

The single-emission CDs with excellent functions have become ideal choice for the analysis and detection of pesticide and veterinary drug residues in food. Simple surface doping or modification will interact with the target analytes and enhance or quench fluorescence. The dual-emission or multi-emission CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor can further realize the fluorescence colorimetric dual model detection, and use the change of the fluorescence colour to establish a convenient visual sensing mechanism in the food safety inspection process. Such as, A fluorescence “turn on” ratiometric fluorescent sensor was used to determine carbendazim in apple and strawberry samples. The sensing process was achieved through the strong fluorescence resonance energy transfer between N-doped CDs and gold nanoclusters. This ratiometric detection of carbendazim was achieved by recording the photoluminescence and second-order Rayleigh scattering signal of the CDs/gold nanocluster system (Y. Yang et al., 2020). Organophosphorus pesticides are widely used and the largest amount of pesticides, residues of varying degrees in crops often cause serious health or economic consequences. Use pancreatin and o-phenylenediamine to obtain N-doped CDs. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase are added to catalyze acetylcholine to generate choline and then further oxidize to H2O2. In the presence of horseradish peroxidase, the interaction of H2O2 and o-phenylenediamine can obtain fluorescence 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), based on the internal filter effect between N-doped CDs and DAP to constructe a ratiometric fluorescence detection platform to obtain good detection results of organophosphorus pesticides such as dichlorvos at 3.2 ppb and methyl parathion at 13 ppb in the form of lower LOD, which were successfully verified in actual water and food samples, the result is satisfactory (Shan, Jiandong, Chu, Xiao, & Zhang, 2019).

In food processing, mycotoxins in cereals and other raw materials cannot be completely destroyed. Thus, the final food product is contaminated by mycotoxins. Common mycotoxins in cereals include aflatoxin, fumonisin, and zearalenone. Microcystin-LR was detected by a fluorescent nanoparticle label-free fluorescence enzyme-linked immunoassay in water and fish samples. Polyclonal antibodies against microcystin-LR were labeled with horseradish peroxidase and used as a signal probe for binding with the analyte in the sample or for coating the antigen. After washing with unbound antibodies, the substrate system was added. The oxidation product of ABTS (ox-ABTS) catalysed by horseradish peroxidase effectively caused the fluorescence quenching of the subsequently added silane-doped CDs, which were used for the detection of microcystin-LR (Xu et al., 2020). Liang et al. proposed a good method for the sensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 in peanut samples by using CD-coated MIP (Liang et al., 2018). Additionally, positively charged N-doped CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of pancreatin and then assembled on aptamer-modified gold nanoparticles by electrostatic interactions for the supersensitive detection of aflatoxin B1. This assay exhibited superhigh sensitivity with a detection limit of 5 pg/mL and a wide range of linear responses from 5 pg/mL to 2.00 ng/mL (Wang et al., 2016).

In addition to the application of single-emission CDs based optical sensors in the detection of food toxins, in food safety analysis, the construction of CDs based dual-emission or multi-emission sensors is expected to break through the “yes/no” qualitative detection and achieve the visual semi-quantitative detection of target toxin analytes (Han, Yang, Luo, He, & Li, 2020). On this basis, it is expected to establish high-quality dual and multi-emission CDs based optical sensor to detect trace toxins in food.

Various metal elements in the environment can enter the human body through food and drinking water intake, respiratory tract inhalation, and skin contact. Some of these metal elements can have toxic effects on the human body at low intakes, such as Cr2+, Pb2+, and Hg+, which often referred to as toxic metals. Most of the heavy metals ingested by the human body are food as a medium. Frequent consumption of heavy metal contaminated food may cause human body poisoning. However, people have realized from practice that the human body's intake of heavy metals is not simply a function of the concentration of heavy metals and the speed of eating, but also related to the type of food. Aiming at the hazards of heavy metals in food, an optical sensor system based on CDs is constructed to carry out effective analysis and detection are worthwhile. Preparation of new cobalt (II) doped CDs, based on the internal filter effect, photoluminescence quenching occurred in the presence of Cr (VI) ions, quickly and successfully detected Cr(VI) ions in tap water and fish samples, effectively reducing the risk of Cr(VI) contamination in water and seafood (H. Y. Zhang, Wang, Xiao, Wang, & Feng, 2017). Metal ions such as Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ cannot be biodegraded, and will have cumulative effect in the human body through the food chain. Ag+ are one of the important detection objects in the quality of aquatic products due to its strong neurotoxicity. After the microwave-assisted reaction of 2-azaimidazole and hydroxyl compound is used to obtain fluorescence emission CDs, the number of hydroxyl groups is adjusted to enhance the emission performance of CDs. CDs with high QY are used as effective chemical sensor for Ag+ analysis and detection in water by static quenching, with high selectivity and LOD of 6.3 nM (Feng, Zhao, Bian, & Tang, 2019). Preparation of thioctic acid-CDs and construction of a fluorescent “turn-off-on” sensor system for simultaneous detection of Hg2+ and thiophanate methyl (TM), with excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability, which proved that the electron transfer effect caused fluorescence “turn-off”, the formation of TM-Hg complexes caused the fluorescence “turn-on” model, which successfully detected Hg2+ and TM in actual tap water, grape juice and reticulated peel water samples (Wang et al., 2021).

The dual-emission CDs based optical sensor system have also been successfully applied to the detection of metal ions in food, effectively overcoming the negative effects of the complexity of the food matrix. The dual-emission CDs-gold nanoclusters (CDs-AuNCs) was constructed. Under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, sensor system showed two well-resolved emission peaks at 440 nm and 655 nm. The fluorescence emission peak of CDs at 440 nm is not sensitive to the presence of Hg2+ in aqueous solution, while the fluorescence emission peak of AuNCs at 655 nm can completely quenched with the interaction between Hg2+ and Au+, and the effective ratiometric fluorescence sensor can clearly distinguish the transition of fluorescence from red to blue with the naked eye as the concentration of Hg2+ increases (Xie et al., 2018). Another interesting study found that the CDs with a single long-wavelength emission peak at the excitation wavelength of 478 nm will have a ratio increase peak at 532 nm due to the addition of Ag+, accompanied by a decrease in the emission at 618 nm (the original emission peak), exhibited ratiometric fluorescence emission characteristics (I618/I532) in the range of 0–140 μM, showed a significant fluorescence change from orange to green, attached to the specific reaction between Ag+ and glutathione, and the green colour of the fluorescence colour returned to orange, the fluorescence change of the sensing system can be recovered according to ratio (Jiao et al., 2019).

Agricultural irrigation water pollution, soil pollution, or pollution during food processing are all sources of Cu2+ in food. The presence of Cu2+ increases the risk of human red blood cell damage. Therefore, it is necessary to develop food safety analysis methods with high detection sensitivity. The independent blue CDs, red and green QDs are mixed to construct a multi-emission CDs based optical sensing system. The blue CDs are used as a blank background, and the double response signal will cause obvious fluorescence quenching due to the presence of Cu2+, realize detection of Cu2+ effectively. At the same time, since the human eye is more sensitive to colour than brightness, a portable fluorescent test paper is prepared based on a multi-emission sensing system to achieve fast and sensitive visual detection of Cu2+ (Cai et al., 2018).

Based on current research, most of the reported CDs contain single-emission fluorescent centres. These CDs were constructed by various means including doping with other atoms, combining with MOFs or COFs, and combining with MIPs, and the produced biosensor systems successfully realized the detection of analytical substances. In recent years, ratiometric fluorescent probes based on CDs could effectively improve the sensitivity and accuracy of this detection method. Compared to the method of functionalizing the surface of CDs and then connecting them with a corresponding fluorescent emitting material, directly using dual emission CDs as the fluorescent material to detect substances was a better choice. Finally, a fluorescence analysis based on CDs adjusted with multiple fluorescence emission peaks was also placed on the agenda. Although optical detection methods based on CDs are constantly being studied, it is still worth paying attention to further developing CD-based materials as signal carriers that are more convenient and environmentally friendly, while also improving the sensitivity and accuracy of detection methods.

First, to cater to the current concept of green environmental protection, we can work on the pre-synthesis of CDs and their subsequent degradation, explore more natural and harmless substances as sources of CDs synthesis or even investigate waste utilization to reduce system damage, and develop biosafe CDs that exhibited both fluorescence detection characteristics and a subsequent degradation property to reduce secondary pollution. Second, CDs can be used in combination with other ligands to improve the accuracy of fluorescence detection. Some new mesoporous materials have the characteristics of a arge adsorption capacity, uniform mesopore structure and high specific surface area. When used in combination with CDs, the detection sensitivity will be effectively improved. For example, CDs can be used in combination with a molecular sieve (MCM-41) to obtain new fluorescent composite materials with both fluorescent properties and porous properties to achieve substance detection (M. Wang et al., 2019). Third, some advanced technologies can be introduced for the fluorescence detection of carbon-based materials to eliminate the deficiencies of traditional methods. For example, recently proposed computer simulation technology can efficiently and accurately design and select the pre-synthesis scheme of CDs and respond to different functional requirements, which lowers the cost input of traditional CD synthesis methods and improves the success rate and accuracy of synthesized CDs. In addition, the combination of a CD-based sensing system and immunoassay technology will also improve the sensitivity and selectivity of CDs based optical sensing materials, thereby extending the application range of detection. The last challenge is that most of the current fluorescent detection systems based on CDs are carried out in the laboratory. We are still eager to develop a standardized fluorescent detection system based on CDs, with commercial or industrial fluorescent detection materials, thereby producing fluorescent sensors with high portability, including fluorescent test strips, fluorescent colour cards, fluorescent test strips and films, and even applications used in everyday mobile phones. On the basis of more effectively overcoming the interference of irrelevant factors and improving the selectivity of detection methods, CDs provide new methods for environmental supervision and food safety detection.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
